moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Polish Opening, a rare but creative first move that immediately stakes a claim on the queenside. By playing b4, we're preparing to fianchetto our light-squared bishop on b2, which will eye the long diagonal toward g1—a setup that can be surprisingly flexible and hard to refute if Black isn't careful. This move is unconventional enough to take opponents out of their preparation, but it does cede the center, so we'll need to be ready to challenge Black's central control later with moves like e3 or c4.

Black meets the Polish with the Dutch Defense, immediately counterattacking in the center and claiming space on the kingside. This move controls the critical e4 and g4 squares, preventing us from establishing a pawn there, and it signals Black's intention to build a kingside presence with moves like e6, developing toward the Dutch's typical pawn structure. However, f5 also weakens Black's king position—the e6 and g5 squares become softer—so we'll want to keep an eye out for tactical opportunities as the game develops.
